There's a master services agreement, which is the 12 page, all the legal jargon stuff that will govern everything that I do with a given client. And then for each project, we fill out a statement of work, and that's just one or two pages going over the nuts and bolts of that project in particular. So you know, what am I delivering? What am I getting paid? What's the due date? That sort of like brass tacks of the project. And then all of the payment terms, all of the legal terms, all of the other termination of the contract, if a client needs to end it or if I need to end it, all that stuff is in the master agreement. ## [12:12 - 15:13]

to approach these. Like this is the stuff when I get a contract from a client. This is where I'm calling my lawyer and saying, Hey, can you look at this? So these, this is more going to be a high level just to give you an idea of again, what I'm talking about, like what these things are and how they look in the day to day of freelancing. So first thing is an independent contractor section. This is where the client, you know, they want to make sure that you're not in an employee, that they don't have to pay benefits. They don't have to pay, you know, payroll taxes. And again, speaking from the US in particular, the client wants to make it clear that you are an independent contractor and you are being hired as such. You are not an employee. You're not a partner. It's not a joint venture, et cetera. So you will see, you know, a section like this will exist in basically every freelance contract that sets those boundaries and guidelines of the relationship between the business and you. So and you'll see stuff like, you know, you're not required to exclusively work for the company. You're not going to be, you know, getting oversight. They're not going to control when and where you work. This is especially in the US is to make it clear that this company in the US in particular, if you are, you know, if it looks like a duck and it quacks like a duck, it is a duck. If you are, if your day to day of your work is at looks like an employee, the government can say, you know, that even though you technically are classified as an independent contractor, in the contract, they can say, no, this person is actually an employee because if you look at their day to day work, they're behaving as an employee. So they want as much coverage as possible showing to prove that you are not an employee, that you truly are independent. So that's why they'll have a lot of stuff like this in the contract. So next section is an intellectual property section. So again, the client wants to make sure that whatever work you produce, they own, right? They're paying you to write them copy. They want to own that copy when you're done. And the way the copyright laws work specifically in the US is once something is created in a fixed and tangible form like typed on a computer, there is a copyright to that, even if it's not registered. And the person who creates the work owns the copyright. So you as the copywriter, when you write your sales letter or your emails, you own that intellectual property, you own the copyright to that email, to that letter, whatever it is. So you need to give those rights to your client in order for them to actually go and use it and profit from it. So every contract is going to have some language like this saying that you will transfer the rights

## [18:14 - 21:28]

Again, this is where, like this is where your lawyer earns their money because the details can get really dicey and complicated. So typically with me as the freelancer, I look for my clients to indemnify me because they're eight figure, nine figure companies and I'm just little Brian writing sales copy. And if they go and they run an app, like I write a sales letter for them, they go, they change something I write so that it becomes non-compliant and then get sued by the FDA or FTC. And in that discovery, they find out that I wrote the copy and the FTC somehow comes after me as well. This would protect me and it would say, the client will take care of my legal costs. So the first section, the first handful of things that we're going to talk about are the ownership of the intellectual property. So anything you do for this project is instantly the client's property. That's not a great scenario for you because the client owns the rights immediately and you have a dispute, then they still own the rights and you can go and start using your copy right away as you work through the dispute. So at that point, you have very little leverage to say, you know, if they still owe you for this, you know, second half of the project or whatever that is, you have very little leverage to say, hey, I still need that last payment. So if you think about it, before the first draft, I get nothing if I cancel or I get 50% if the client cancels or breaches the agreement to the point where I have to cancel. Make sense with me so far? Right. So then similarly, after the first draft, if I cancel the project after the first draft, I get 50% because again, once the first draft is done, that's the bulk of the project. So if I

## [39:41 - 42:54]

have to tell the client, listen, here's your first draft, I can't bring this to a conclusion. So I'm only going to take half the fee, but you've got a full sales letter, first draft, they can polish that up pretty quickly and get it running. And compared to paying 100% of the fee, only paying 50%, that's a fairly reasonable scenario for them. At the same time, if I finish a first draft and then the client cancels or again, they do something to breach the agreement that forces me to cancel, then I get 75%. So that way, as you see in both scenarios, whoever cancels the project or breaches the agreement to the point where the other party has to cancel, they quote unquote lose money. Right? If I cancel before delivering a first draft, I lose everything, I have to give a full refund. And if we're done with the first draft, and the client decides to cancel before we get to finish, I get 75% of the whole project, as opposed to 50%, if I'm the one who cancels at that point. So whoever's cancelling bears more of the cost than whoever's not cancelling. So does that make sense? Let me see, pop up in the chat. Any questions so far on these payment terms? Okay. confidential information. So I would make sure to just have one quick sentence in there that says, notwithstanding the foregoing, which means like, despite everything we just said, I am allowed to do this one thing, which is share the drafts of the deliverables from any statement of work for feedback within the private copy accelerator mastermind group. That way, if I post something in the group for feedback, if a client happens to be in the group, for some of us who are working with clients who are in copy accelerator, they see that post, they could get pissed. those bases so that you can't get, so it balances it. ## [49:02 - 52:03]

of work for me, right? So this is sort of we've transitioned now from the master services agreement, the larger legal discussion to now what are the terms of the particular project that would be included in a statement of work. So for a statement of work for a fixed price project, I would include first a 14 day limit on the review cycles. So if you think about it, if your contract says, you know, you get half of the payment up front and then half when the client approves your copy, if you wind up in a situation where they just ghost, A, you're never getting paid and B, you know, that project technically remains incomplete and like open forever, right? Unless the, you know, the old, because if the only thing in the contract that says the project is over is the client approving the copy or publishing the copy, then if they never do that, this project is just open indefinitely. So that's a problem. And then again, on the payment thing, you know, if the client, if you don't get paid your second half until the client is approving the copy, you know, they disappear for three months and all of a sudden you're not getting paid for three months. So these two sections address those, that combined problem. Actually, let's start with the second one first. So down here, it says you're going to invoice the client for half up front and then half upon approval or month, day, year, whichever is sooner. That way, you know, again, if the client ghosts, but you have a hard date where it's like, no matter what the second payment comes January 15th, 2021 client goes in the middle of the project. Well, January 15th rolls along and you can send that second invoice and it's in the contract. You get your second payment and you're cool. At least you don't have to worry about the money side of, you know, of that equation. But if you only had this second part where you got that invoice on, you know, the second half on that fixed date, the client could come back three months later and say, Oh, hey, we want to pick this project up again. And if there's nothing to like, automatically close the project, if they disappear, you're contractually obligated to finish the project at that point, which is, it kind of sucks because you'd still, you know, you would have booked new clients at that point, you would be, you would be in a bad spot. So this, this first section says the client has 14 days to request revisions. And if after 14 days, they have not responded, not asked for any revisions, then the project is automatically approved. So that means that in that scenario, the client ghosts

## [52:04 - 55:08]

will 14 days after they don't provide feedback. That's it. Project is over. If they want to come back and finish it, you can legitimately charge them again. If they do, you then work on the revisions, submit your, you know, your second draft, they have another 14 days on that draft. First, it does not say I must provide 10 hours of work or whatever. Like it doesn't say that

## [55:08 - 58:13]

there is a minimum. So it's just not to go over a certain amount. But if it's zero, right, the client just doesn't ask me to do anything for a month, I would still get paid my entire fee because I held the space on the calendar for them. I was available if they needed me. They chose not to use me. That's fine. I still get paid everything because there's no minimum requirement in terms of what I have to do. But if the client keeps coming at me with more and more and more work, there is that maximum where it's not to exceed a certain number of hours per month unless agreed in advance. Now that last piece agreed in advance is key because I tell my clients just sort of on the side in conversation that as long as we're like, as long as they're cool and they're not like, they're respecting my time and they're not constantly slamming me with more work than I can take, I tell them that I will always agree in advance to go over if they need me to, you know, if they got a big launch coming up one month and they need a little extra time for me, like I'm not going to nickel and dine them for that. So that's sort of how I handle that sort of on the relationship side. But in the contract, this is what it says for those reasons. Sound good? Okay. And then the next thing is this landlord retainer. So I think a lot of you have, or many of you have probably seen this already. So the name comes from the fact that in New York City in particular, landlords are really, really aggressive in terms of what they collect upfront before you can rent an apartment. So they get the first month's rent, they get, you know, security deposit, they get like an application fee and they get the last month's rent, which is key because that means if you leave the apartment and you break the lease, they've got that last month's payment to be able to cover the time till they find a new tenant. So I took that idea. And I do the same thing in my retainers, where if I'm going to start a retainer, they pay for their last month upfront. And that way, if they disappear in the middle of the retainer or they cancel last minute, I've got a whole month of money to be able to cover myself until the, you know, until I can find a new retainer client. So basically, this section says, you know, what's your monthly fee, you're going to submit invoices on the 15th of the month, and then the payment is due by the last day of the month. So it's like that. Make sure you're always getting paid a month in advance. So, you know, the client, you know, again, it's what, it's December, right? So I had invoices go out in December 15th that are due December 31st for me to do work in January. If any of my clients missed those invoices, I would have the right to not work in January until they made that payment. So that's just make sure that you're not doing work without first getting paid on a month to month basis. But, you know, again, the problem is if you're doing that,

you know, January 1st comes and goes, January 3rd comes and goes, client hasn't paid you, and they were like, all right, we're done. Sorry.
